30:0.rendering: The words of King Lemuel, the oracle that his mother taught him.
30:0.note: The Hebrew word for "oracle" here is massa, the same loaded term used for the weighty prophetic burdens in Isaiah and Nahum, signaling that this is no ordinary motherly advice but a prophetic word passed from a queen mother to her son. It subtly echoes the unified biblical pattern of wisdom being handed down along family lines (think Deuteronomy 6 and the king in Deuteronomy 17), and it sets up the portrait of the ideal royal household that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the true King shaped by divine wisdom.

30:1.rendering: What are you doing, my son? What are you doing, son of my womb? What are you doing, son of my vows?
30:1.note: The Hebrew word for "son" (bar) here is an Aramaic loanword, creating a striking wordplay with "my vows" (nadarai) that echoes the mother's lifelong dedication, much like Hannah's vow in 1 Samuel 1. This intimate maternal plea opens the oracle of King Lemuel, a non-Israelite ruler, showing how the Bible's wisdom tradition draws from the nations and ultimately points forward to the perfect Son who fulfills every vow of covenant faithfulness.

30:2.rendering: Do not give your strength to women, or your ways to those who destroy kings.
30:2.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for a warrior's might or a king's vigor, creating a clever wordplay that echoes the warnings in Deuteronomy 17 about kings who multiply wives, horses, and wealth. This verse sits inside a mother's instruction to her royal son, part of the larger biblical pattern exploring how covenant faithfulness or its failure shapes the story of Israel's kings, ultimately pointing forward to the true King whose strength is perfected in self-giving love.

30:3.rendering: It is not for kings, Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to crave strong drink,
30:3.note: The Hebrew word for "drink" (shathah) echoes the lush, celebratory banquets of wisdom literature, but here it's flipped into a warning: kings who rule on behalf of the vulnerable must stay clear-headed so justice doesn't get blurred by excess. This royal caution threads through the Bible's story of flawed rulers, from Saul and David to the ideal king envisioned in Isaiah 11, ultimately finding its fulfillment in Jesus, the clear-eyed servant-king who brings true justice without being mastered by anything.

30:4.rendering: Lest they drink and forget what is decreed, and pervert the rights of all the afflicted.
30:4.note: The Hebrew word for "decreed" here is the same one used for the king's legal judgments and the Torah's instructions, creating a wordplay that links the king's duty to uphold justice with God's own covenant faithfulness. This warning about royal drunkenness echoes the concern for the vulnerable that runs from the Torah's commands to protect the poor, through the prophets' cries against injustice, all the way to Jesus, who came as the true king who would not forget or pervert the rights of the afflicted.

30:5.rendering: Give strong drink to the one who is perishing, and wine to the bitter in soul.
30:5.note: The Hebrew word for "perishing" here echoes the language of those on the brink of death or ruin, a motif that runs through Proverbs contrasting the path of wisdom with the way that leads to destruction. This verse sits in a royal instruction on justice, inviting the king to provide comfort to the suffering while reserving clear thinking for himself, a thread that finds deeper resonance in the story of a king who gives himself as wine for the world's deepest bitterness.

30:6.rendering: Let them drink and forget their poverty, and remember their misery no more.
30:6.note: The Hebrew word for "misery" here is the same one used throughout the Bible for the crushing hardship of exile and oppression, creating an echo of the people's desperate need for a deliverer, a thread that runs from the groans of Israel in Egypt all the way to the arrival of the one who ultimately lifts the burden of our deepest poverty.

30:7.rendering: Open your mouth for the voiceless, for the justice of all the children of the destitute.
30:7.note: The Hebrew phrase "bnei mishpat" literally means "children of passing away" or "sons of the perishing," a vivid image for those whose lives are slipping away without anyone to speak for them. This call to use your voice for the powerless echoes the biblical story's deep concern for the vulnerable, from the exodus deliverance to the prophets' cries for justice, and finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, who became the advocate for the voiceless and invited his followers into that same mission.

30:8.rendering: Speak up, and give justice to the poor and needy. Judge rightly in their cause.
30:8.note: The Hebrew word for "judge rightly" (din) here echoes back to the very beginning of Israel's story, where God "judged" the cause of the oppressed in Egypt and brought them out. This call to the kingly figure in Proverbs 31 is part of the Bible's larger pattern of image-of-God people who reflect the Creator's passion for the vulnerable, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the true royal Son who advocates for the least of these.

30:9.rendering: A wife of noble character, who can find? She is far more precious than jewels.
30:9.note: The Hebrew word for "noble character" here is *chayil*, the same term used for a "mighty warrior" or "man of valor" throughout the Hebrew Bible, like in the stories of Gideon or David. This poem paints the ideal woman not as a passive figure but as a strong, resourceful partner who brings shalom to her household, echoing the image-of-God partnership in Genesis 1 and 2, and ultimately pointing forward to the kind of wisdom and faithfulness that finds its fulfillment in Jesus' bride, the church.

30:10.rendering: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he will have no lack of gain.
30:10.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" (shalal) often refers to plunder or spoil won in battle, creating a subtle wordplay: this woman's husband can rest secure because she wages war on behalf of their household, turning potential loss into abundance. This image of a trustworthy partner echoes the Bible's larger theme of covenant faithfulness, where God's people are called to bring order and provision out of chaos, a thread that ultimately finds its fulfillment in Jesus, the bridegroom who fully trusts and provides for his bride, the church.

30:11.rendering: She does him good, and not harm, all the days of her life.
30:11.note: The Hebrew word for "good" here (tob) echoes the repeated refrain in Genesis 1 where God declares creation "good," and it resonates with the ideal of covenant faithfulness that runs from the garden to the prophets' vision of a renewed people. This verse sits at the climax of Proverbs, painting a portrait of wisdom embodied in a woman who brings life and order to her household, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus as the true image of divine wisdom.

30:12.rendering: She seeks out wool and flax, and works with willing hands.
30:12.note: The Hebrew word for "seeks" (d-r-sh) is the same one used for diligently searching out wisdom or even seeking God himself, while "works with willing hands" literally says her palms take hold with delight. This picture of the capable woman echoes the creation ideal of humans as image-bearing partners who creatively order the raw materials of the good world, a thread that runs from Genesis 1-2 through the wisdom literature and finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the truly wise one who works with delight to bring order and life to the world.

30:13.rendering: She is like the ships of the merchant, she brings her food from far away.
30:13.note: The Hebrew word for "merchant" here is the same one used for the traders who brought exotic goods from distant lands in Solomon's day, painting the capable woman as an active participant in the ancient economy of abundance. This image echoes the larger biblical theme of wisdom ordering creation into fruitful provision, much like the Proverbs 8 portrait of Lady Wisdom working alongside God at the dawn of time, and it ultimately points forward to how Jesus embodies and multiplies that same wise, far-reaching provision for his people.

30:14.rendering: She rises while it is still night, and gives food to her household, and a portion to her servant girls.
30:14.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" here is the same one used for the daily bread God provided Israel in the wilderness and for the "daily bread" Jesus teaches us to pray for, echoing the wisdom of a woman who participates in God's faithful provision for his household.

30:15.rendering: She considers a field and acquires it, from the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.
30:15.note: The Hebrew word for "considers" (zamam) is the same one used in the Torah when God "plans" or "purposes" something (like in Genesis 50:20 or Jeremiah 29:11), portraying this woman as an image-of-God figure who thoughtfully rules and creates abundance. Her actions echo the ancient ideal of covenant faithfulness, turning wilderness into fruitful vineyard, a theme that runs from Eden through Isaiah's songs and finds its climax in Jesus, the true vine whose people bear fruit that lasts.

30:16.rendering: She wraps her loins with strength, and she makes her arms powerful.
30:16.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for God's power in the exodus and for the royal might of a king in Psalm 18, linking this wise woman to the image-of-God vocation of ruling creation with creative, life-giving power.

30:17.rendering: She sees that her trading is good, and her lamp does not go out at night.
30:17.note: The Hebrew word for "sees" (ta'am) literally means "tastes," creating a beautiful wordplay: this wise woman tastes that her trading yields good results, echoing the earlier image of the woman who "tastes and sees" that the Lord is good (Psalm 34:8). Her lamp staying lit through the night evokes the ancient practice of keeping light burning as a symbol of ongoing vigilance and provision, connecting to the Bible's larger theme of God's people as lights that don't go out in darkness, a thread that ultimately points forward to Jesus as the light that the darkness cannot overcome.

30:18.rendering: She stretches out her hands toward the distaff, and her hands grasp the spindle.
30:18.note: The Hebrew word for "hands" (yad) here echoes the capable "hands" of the strong woman in verse 17, painting a picture of one who works with both strength and skill, like the skilled artisans in Exodus 35 whose hands were filled by God's Spirit to craft the tabernacle. This image of creative, productive labor weaves into the Bible's larger story of humans as image-bearers who partner with God to bring order and beauty out of raw material, a thread that runs from Eden's garden to the new creation where God's people will reign with renewed creativity.

30:19.rendering: She opens her palm to the poor, and she reaches out her hands to the needy.
30:19.note: The Hebrew word for "palm" here is the same one used for the hand of generosity in passages like Deuteronomy 15, where Israel is called to open their hand to the poor as an act of covenant faithfulness. This woman embodies the wisdom of the fear of the Lord by extending heaven-and-earth overlap, practically imaging God's care for the vulnerable in a way that echoes throughout the biblical story from the Torah's commands to the early church's shared life, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus who opened his hands on the cross for the needy.

30:20.rendering: She is not afraid of the snow for her household, for all her household is clothed in scarlet.
30:20.note: The Hebrew word for "scarlet" (shanim) carries a double meaning, it sounds like the word for "double" (shenayim), hinting that her family is doubly protected, both physically warm and dressed in the vibrant, expensive dye that signals honor and security. This image of a wise woman providing for her household echoes the Proverbs 31 acrostic's larger portrait of covenant faithfulness, the kind of wisdom that creates a little outpost of shalom amid life's storms, a thread that runs from the capable woman of the Hebrew Bible all the way to the bride of the Lamb who is clothed in fine linen, bright and pure (Revelation 19:8).

30:21.rendering: She makes coverings for herself, her clothing is fine linen and purple.
30:21.note: The Hebrew word for "coverings" (marbaddim) echoes the tabernacle curtains and priestly garments, while "fine linen and purple" are the very materials used in God's sacred tent and the high priest's robes, quietly portraying this woman of valor as one who weaves heaven-and-earth beauty into everyday life.

30:22.rendering: Her husband is respected at the city gate, where he sits among the elders of the land.
30:22.note: The Hebrew word for "respected" here is noda, carrying the sense of being known and honored in the public square, the ancient place of justice and decision-making. This verse echoes the wisdom ideal of a household that brings shalom to the community, a thread running from the garden mandate to image God faithfully all the way to the new-creation people whose lives make the city gates ring with honor, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus who sits at the right hand as the truly honored one.

30:23.rendering: She makes linen garments and sells them, and supplies merchants with sashes.
30:23.note: The Hebrew word for "sash" (or belt) here is the same one used for the high priest's ornate waistband in Exodus 28 and 39, an item of beauty and dignity that marked out sacred service. This woman of noble character works with her hands to create value that extends far beyond her household, echoing the image-of-God vocation to bring order, beauty, and blessing into the world, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus who clothes his people with his own righteousness and dignity.

30:24.rendering: She is clothed with strength and dignity, and she laughs at the days to come.
30:24.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here (oz) echoes the same term used for the virtuous woman in Proverbs 31:17 who "girds herself with oz," linking her to the powerful, wise woman of the poem. This image of clothing oneself with strength and then laughing at the future reverses the anxiety of exile and uncertainty found throughout the Hebrew Bible, pointing forward to the confident joy that Jesus' followers experience as they put on the new self renewed in God's image.

30:25.rendering: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.
30:25.note: The Hebrew word for "teaching" here is torah, the same term used for God's own instruction that shapes Israel into a wise and gracious people. This woman embodies the wisdom literature's vision of a life that overflows with both shrewd insight and covenant kindness, echoing how the fear of the Lord produces a character that reflects God's own torah-shaped mercy, a thread that runs from the wise woman of Proverbs to the community formed by Jesus, the living Word.

30:26.rendering: She watches over the goings-on of her household, and the bread of idleness she does not eat.
30:26.note: The Hebrew word for "watches" (tsophiyah) is the same root used for the lookout in a watchtower, portraying this woman as an alert guardian of her household's life and rhythms. This verse weaves into Proverbs' larger theme of wisdom versus folly, where diligent attention to the details of daily life echoes the fear of the Lord that begins wisdom, ultimately pointing forward to how Jesus embodies and empowers a life of alert, fruitful stewardship in God's renewed household.

30:27.rendering: Her children rise up and call her happy, her husband too, and he praises her.
30:27.note: The Hebrew word for "call her happy" (ashreha) is the same root as the "blessed" or "happy" declarations that open the book of Psalms and the Beatitudes in Matthew 5, creating an echo that portrays the capable woman as the living embodiment of covenant wisdom that brings flourishing to her household.

30:28.rendering: Many daughters have done noble things, but you surpass them all.
30:28.note: The Hebrew word for "noble things" here is the same one used for the "valiant warrior" or "mighty man of valor" throughout the Hebrew Bible, creating a brilliant wordplay: this woman of strength and wisdom is portrayed with the language of a battle hero. In the bigger story, she echoes the image-of-God vocation to rule and subdue with creative goodness, pointing forward to Jesus who perfectly embodies both wisdom and strength as the true Valiant One.

30:29.rendering: Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD, she is the one to be praised.
30:29.note: The Hebrew word for "praise" here (halal) echoes the very last word of the book of Psalms, turning this portrait of a wise woman into a living psalm of praise, while "fears the LORD" links her back to the foundational theme of Proverbs: the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom. This verse quietly points forward to the New Testament's vision of the people of God as a radiant bride, whose true beauty comes not from outward appearance but from covenant faithfulness to her king.

30:30.rendering: Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the city gates.
30:30.note: This closing line echoes the poem's opening call to "find" a woman of noble character, forming a beautiful frame around the acrostic, while the phrase "fruit of her hands" recalls the garden imagery of Genesis 1-3 and the wisdom tradition's promise that the fear of the Lord leads to a life whose deeds speak for themselves at the city gates where justice and community life happen.
